one_line_claim: The paper claims that a small amount of test-time LLM relevance feedback can refine only the query embedding, letting standard embedding models behave more like task-adaptive zero-shot retrievers/classifiers over a full corpus.
what_they_tested: They optimize the query vector to match an LLM teacher's relevance-score distribution over the initial top-K documents, then rescore the full corpus with the refined vector. Main experiments use five embedding models, Mistral-Small-3.2-24B as teacher, K=20, T=100, LR=1e-4, and MAP on REALSCHOLARQUERY, CLINC150, ARGKP-21, and FOLLOWIR; appendices add BANKING77, NFCORPUS, ReFIT, HyDE, alternate teachers, instruction templates, K sensitivity, and feedback-quality correlations.
strongest_point: The rerank-only control is the best evidence: for most embedding models, the same 20 LLM judgments help more when used to move the query vector and rescore the whole corpus than when used only to reorder the judged top-20 documents. That makes the method more than a trivial top-K LLM reranker.
weakest_point: The paper sells a full-corpus classification/search system, but mostly evaluates ranking MAP and explicitly defers threshold selection. It also underplays the real online bottleneck: every query still needs 20 LLM query-document judgments, while the latency table excludes those calls.
missing_baselines: A same-budget LLM or cross-encoder reranker over top-20, top-100, and top-N candidates, with MAP, recall, latency, and cost reported alongside query refinement.
A binary classifier baseline trained from the same 20 LLM pseudo-labels, such as logistic regression, linear SVM, prototype/centroid scoring, or a calibrated score threshold over document embeddings.
Classic pseudo-relevance feedback baselines such as Rocchio/vector averaging using top documents, with and without LLM-labeled positives and negatives.
LLM query rewriting/query expansion baselines beyond HyDE, especially discriminative rewrites that include exclusions, constraints, or positive/negative examples.
An active-sampling baseline that asks the LLM to label diverse or uncertain documents rather than blindly taking the initial top-K.
A practical thresholding baseline that turns the original or refined ranking into final binary labels, since the stated use case is full-corpus separation.
A same-cost setwise or listwise LLM scorer, since the authors themselves suggest this could make feedback more efficient.
missing_ablations: Top-K composition: top-ranked only versus diversified, random, uncertainty-based, hard-negative, or active-learning selected feedback sets.
Failure stratification by initial top-K recall, number of positives in the feedback set, class imbalance, and query difficulty.
Learning-rate, step-count, optimizer, loss, softmax-temperature, and score-normalization sweeps across all models, not just borrowing one fixed configuration from prior work.
Teacher prompt and calibration sensitivity, including yes/no token variants, listwise scoring, task-instruction wording, and domain-specialized teachers.
Oracle-teacher or gold-label feedback upper bounds to show how much headroom comes from the refinement algorithm versus teacher errors.
Multiple seeds or repeated LLM calls to quantify variance from optimization, model nondeterminism, and prompt/logprob extraction.
End-to-end thresholding ablations for precision/recall/F1 or cost-to-target-recall, not only MAP ranking quality.
Embedding-space sanity checks: whether optimized vectors drift off the natural query manifold, harm nearest-neighbor interpretability, or create brittle adversarial directions.
red_flags: The headline practicality claim depends on 20 LLM relevance calls per query; the measured 79ms query-optimization latency explicitly excludes those calls.
The paper's final objective is binary separation over a corpus, but the evaluation avoids the hard part of choosing per-query thresholds and reports ranking MAP instead.
The feedback set is selected from the initial embedding top-K, so the method is structurally vulnerable when the initial retriever misses positives or returns an uninformative cluster.
CLINC150 query descriptions are generated by GPT-OSS-120B from 10 positive examples, so that benchmark is not a clean test of arbitrary user-written ad-hoc queries.

The claimed consistency has an important exception: Linq-Embed-Mistral gets only about +0.5% average gain on the full six-dataset table, and optimized is not better than rerank-only in the significance table.
Some statistically significant rows appear practically tiny, especially for Linq-Embed-Mistral; effect sizes and confidence intervals would be more informative than p-values alone.

The ReFIT comparison is useful but not definitive: their own text says ReFIT's cross-encoder, normalization, and learning-rate choices do not transfer well, so it is not a strong tuned prior-work baseline for this setting.
The massive-corpus story is only partly tested; the latency example is over CLINC150 and the paper does not give large-scale ANN throughput or wall-clock including LLM feedback.
positive_signals: The algorithm is simple, query-local, and does not require changing embedding-model weights or reindexing document embeddings.
The task framing is practically relevant: literature search, intent analytics, key-point matching, and instruction-sensitive retrieval are good stress tests for dense embeddings.
The main MAP table shows meaningful gains for Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-8B, Llama-Embed-Nemotron-8B, and E5-Mistral-7B across the four main datasets.
The rerank-only baseline directly probes whether the benefit propagates beyond the documents sent to the LLM.
The appendix goes beyond a single table: ReFIT, HyDE, alternate LLM teachers, instruction templates, added datasets, K sensitivity, and feedback-quality correlations are all useful checks.
The authors are appropriately explicit that top-K feedback selection, teacher quality, and inference cost remain limitations or future work.
The LLM feedback scoring procedure is concrete: yes/no prompts with logprob-derived probabilities, not vague natural-language critiques.
The metadata reports a public code release, making this a good candidate for independent replication.
verdict: Worth covering as a solid applied retrieval/classification paper, not as a breakthrough. The episode angle is strong: can a handful of LLM judgments bend an embedding space enough to make cheap retrieval behave like task-adaptive reasoning? The answer is often yes in these benchmarks, but the review should keep pressing on LLM-call cost, thresholding, top-K selection bias, and whether MAP gains become reliable full-corpus decisions.
citations: data/papers/arxiv-2605.12487.md:1-22 gives the title, metadata, abstract claims, +25% improvement claim, and code-release link.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:7-22 states the core claim: LLM-guided query refinement over a small document set improves embedding representations and is positioned as cheaper than corpus-scale LLM pipelines.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:128-149 defines the binary separation problem and notes that thresholding is not addressed there.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:152-200 describes the algorithm: initial embedding ranking, LLM teacher scores over top-K, KL optimization of the query vector, full-corpus rescoring, and no model-weight or document-embedding updates.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:206-257 describes the evaluated tasks: REALSCHOLARQUERY, CLINC150, ARGKP-21, FOLLOWIR, plus BANKING77 and NFCORPUS in the appendix.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:258-309 states that the main metric is MAP over rankings and that final threshold selection is left for future work.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:310-327 gives implementation details: Adam, LR=1e-4, T=100, K=20 top-ranked documents, five embedding models, Mistral-Small teacher, cosine similarity, and logprob-based LLM scores.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:332-351 reports the main MAP table, including large gains for several models and the weak Linq-Embed-Mistral optimized result.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:355-382 explains the rerank-only control, the claim that refinement improves beyond top-K, and the HyDE comparison.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:385-395 reports latency numbers and explicitly says query optimization excludes LLM calls while adding latency for K=20 feedback scores.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:431-459 discusses K sensitivity, teacher-feedback quality, and instruction-template sensitivity.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:477-485 lists limitations around teacher bias/errors, top-K selection missing informative documents, class imbalance, and teacher cost tradeoffs.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:761-789 gives dataset sizes and explains that CLINC150 intent queries were rephrased by GPT-OSS-120B from positive examples.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:814-842 reports the empirical ReFIT comparison and notes why ReFIT choices did not transfer well.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:913-950 reports full six-dataset MAP results with HyDE and optimized variants, and describes the yes/no logprob LLM feedback prompt.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:1140-1166 reports full MAP and significance tables, including non-significant optimized-over-rerank comparisons for Linq-Embed-Mistral and several other cells.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12487.txt:1167-1200 reports alternate LLM teacher results and modest feedback-quality/performance-gain correlations.
